name: Bodhisattva Path
shortDescription: The Bodhisattva Path is the path of practice undertaken by bodhisattvas, those who aspire to achieve Buddhahood for the benefit of all sentient beings. It involves cultivating wisdom, compassion, and skillful means to alleviate suffering and guide others towards enlightenment.
description: The Bodhisattva Path is a central tenet of Mahayana Buddhism, representing the journey towards complete enlightenment undertaken not for personal liberation alone, but for the liberation of all beings. A bodhisattva, in this context, is an individual who has generated bodhicitta, the aspiration to attain Buddhahood for the sake of all sentient beings. This path involves a profound commitment to ethical conduct, mental discipline, and the development of wisdom. Key aspects of the Bodhisattva Path include the practice of the Six Perfections (Paramitas): generosity (dana), ethical conduct (sila), patience (kshanti), effort (virya), concentration (dhyana), and wisdom (prajna). These perfections are cultivated over countless lifetimes, gradually purifying defilements and accumulating merit and wisdom. The path emphasizes the importance of compassion (karuna) and loving-kindness (metta) as fundamental motivations for action. A bodhisattva actively engages in skillful means (upaya) to guide others towards liberation, adapting their teachings and actions to the specific needs and capacities of different individuals. The Bodhisattva Path is not a linear progression but rather a cyclical process of learning, practice, and selfless service. It involves confronting and transforming negative emotions, developing profound insight into the nature of reality, and dedicating oneself to the well-being of all beings without exception. The ultimate goal is to embody the qualities of a Buddha, thereby becoming a source of wisdom, compassion, and guidance for the world. The path highlights the interconnectedness of all beings and the potential for everyone to awaken to their true nature.

sources.title: The Bodhisattva Doctrine in Buddhist Theology
sources.author: Dayal, Har
